question: Walk me through your HR and Leave Management System project.
answer:
Project Overview

I built the HR and Leave Management System at VisionQuest Solutions as a Full Stack Developer from January 2025 to February 2026. It's an internal HRMS platform used by the company for managing employee leave requests, attendance tracking, onboarding, and org-wide analytics.

Architecture

My Key Contributions

  1. 3-Level Approval Chain: I designed the leave approval workflow where an employee submits a request, it goes to their Manager for first approval, then to HR for final approval. I used Angular services with RxJS observables to track state transitions and Fastify endpoints with PostgreSQL transactions to ensure atomicity.
  2. 12+ REST API Endpoints: Designed with Fastify and Prisma ORM. Key business rules I implemented:
    • No leave on weekends (automatic rejection)
    • No backdating of leave requests
    • Automatic leave balance deduction on approval
    • Overlap conflict detection (can't apply for same dates twice)
  3. Real-time Notifications: Used Socket.io integrated with Angular's NgZone so employees get instant approval/rejection notifications without refreshing the page. When a manager approves, the employee's dashboard updates in real-time.
  4. Role-Based Admin Panel: Built with Angular route guards. HR can manage onboarding workflows, configure leave policies per department, set holiday calendars, and view org-wide analytics dashboards.
  5. AI-Assisted Development: Used Claude AI for PostgreSQL query optimization (complex aggregation queries for analytics were initially slow — Claude helped me restructure JOINs and add proper indexes). Also generated realistic test data for 500+ employee records.

Technical Decisions & Trade-offs

DecisionChoiceWhy
Backend frameworkFastify over Express2-3x faster, built-in schema validation, better plugin system
ORMPrisma over raw SQLType-safe queries, auto-generated migrations, easier for team onboarding
RealtimeSocket.io over pollingInstant notifications critical for approval workflows; polling would add 30s delay
AuthJWT with role claimsStateless, works across Render deployment; roles embedded in token reduce DB lookups

Challenges & How I Solved Them

Challenge 1: Overlap Detection. When an employee applies for March 10-15, the system must check if they already have approved/pending leave overlapping those dates. I wrote a PostgreSQL query using date range overlap: WHERE start_date <= $endDate AND end_date >= $startDate with a transaction to prevent race conditions.

Challenge 2: Socket.io + Angular NgZone. Socket events fired outside Angular's zone, so the UI didn't update. I wrapped the Socket listener inside NgZone.run() to trigger change detection.

Challenge 3: Analytics Performance. The dashboard aggregation queries (monthly leave trends, department-wise breakdown) were slow with 500+ employees × 12 months of data. Used Claude AI to help optimize — added composite indexes on (employee_id, start_date) and restructured the query to use GROUP BY with date_trunc instead of application-level aggregation.

Impact

  • Reduced leave approval turnaround from 2 days (email-based) to under 2 hours
  • Eliminated double-booking issues with overlap detection
  • Ran KT sessions to onboard 2 junior developers who independently integrated the leave module into other company systems

question: Walk me through your CRM Sales Dashboard project.
answer:
Project Overview

I worked on the CRM Sales Dashboard at VisionQuest Solutions as a Frontend Developer from March 2024 to December 2024. This was a B2B CRM product used by the company's sales teams to manage leads, track deal pipelines, monitor revenue targets, and generate reports.

Architecture

My Key Contributions

  1. Sales Pipeline Dashboard: Built the main dashboard using Angular 18 with component-based architecture. It displays deal stages (Lead → Qualified → Proposal → Won/Lost), monthly revenue trend charts using Recharts, and target-vs-actual metrics with live data refresh. Each metric card is a reusable component.
  2. Multi-Step Lead Creation Form: Designed using Angular Reactive Forms with 4 steps: Basic Info → Contact Details → Deal Configuration → Review & Submit. Features include:
    • Field-level validation with inline error messages
    • Conditional fields (e.g., showing 'Company Size' only for B2B leads)
    • Role-based visibility — Admins see all fields, Managers see team fields, Agents see only their own
  3. Performance Optimization: Implemented lazy-loaded Angular Router modules (reduced initial bundle by 40%) and OnPush change detection strategy. For the deals list with 10K+ records, I implemented virtualised list rendering using CDK Virtual Scroll.
  4. Reusable Component Library: Built data tables (with sorting, filtering, pagination), modals (confirmation + form modals), form inputs (with validation wrappers), and chart cards — all using Angular Material + Tailwind CSS. These components were used consistently across the entire CRM product.
  5. API Integration Layer: Integrated 15+ REST APIs using Angular HttpClient with interceptors for:
    • JWT token auto-refresh on 401 responses
    • Global error handling with toast notifications
    • Loading state management (automatic spinner on API calls)

Technical Decisions & Trade-offs

DecisionChoiceWhy
Change detectionOnPush strategyDashboard has many components; Default strategy caused unnecessary re-renders, dropped FPS
List renderingCDK Virtual Scroll10K+ deals can't render all DOM nodes; virtual scroll keeps only visible items in DOM
StylingAngular Material + TailwindMaterial for complex widgets (tables, dialogs), Tailwind for layout and spacing — best of both
State managementServices + BehaviorSubjectTeam was small, app wasn't complex enough to warrant NgRx overhead

Challenges & How I Solved Them

Challenge 1: JWT Token Refresh Race Condition. Multiple API calls firing simultaneously would all get 401s and all try to refresh the token at once. I implemented a token refresh queue using RxJS — the first 401 triggers a refresh, subsequent calls wait for the same refresh observable using shareReplay(1).

Challenge 2: Form Performance. The multi-step form with 30+ fields was sluggish. Profiling showed Angular's form value change subscribers were firing on every keystroke across all steps. I split into FormGroups per step and only subscribed to the active step's changes.

Impact: Initial load time improved by 40% with lazy loading. The reusable component library reduced new feature development time by roughly 25% since developers could compose pages from existing components.

question: Walk me through your ContactsHub personal project.
answer:
Project Overview

ContactsHub is a full-stack contact management platform I solo-built and deployed as a personal project. What makes it special is that I built it across three frontend generations — first in Vanilla JS, then Angular 18, and finally React 18 — demonstrating my progression across all three frontend paradigms. The React version is the production version deployed on Render.

Architecture

Key Features I Built

  1. Full CRUD with Advanced Search: Create, read, update, delete contacts with 8 search filters (name, email, phone, company, city, tags, favorites, groups). Built the search API with MongoDB dynamic query building.
  2. Bulk CSV Upload (500 records/batch): Users can upload a CSV file and the system parses, validates, and inserts up to 500 contacts per batch. I implemented streaming CSV parsing on the backend to handle large files without memory issues.
  3. Canvas API Image Crop Pipeline: Built a client-side image cropping tool using the Canvas API — circular overlay for profile photos, auto-compression to 400×400 JPEG before uploading to Cloudinary. This reduced storage size by 60% compared to uploading raw images.
  4. PWA with Offline Support: Converted the app to a Progressive Web App with Service Worker caching. Users can browse cached contacts offline. Used cache-first strategy for static assets and network-first for API data.
  5. 60+ Playwright E2E Tests: Comprehensive test suite covering all CRUD operations, search filters, CSV upload flow, image crop, and edge cases. Achieved 100% pass rate. Tests run as part of the CI/CD pipeline on Render.
  6. UI Features: Grid/list view toggle, favorites-first sorting, dynamic contact groups, and a premium dark mode — all built with shadcn/ui + Tailwind CSS.

The Three-Generation Story (Interview Gold)

I tell interviewers: 'I built ContactsHub three times. The Vanilla JS version taught me fundamentals — DOM manipulation, event delegation, fetch API. The Angular version taught me enterprise patterns — modules, services, dependency injection, RxJS. The React version taught me modern composition — hooks, context, lazy loading. Each rewrite was intentional, not a restart.'

VersionWhat I LearnedKey Difference
Vanilla JSDOM fundamentals, event handling, no-framework thinkingEverything manual — templating, routing, state
Angular 18Enterprise patterns, DI, modules, TypeScript strictnessOpinionated structure, built-in solutions
React 18Composition over configuration, hooks, ecosystem flexibilityChoose-your-own architecture, faster iteration

Challenges & Solutions

Challenge 1: Canvas Image Crop Cross-Browser. The circular crop overlay worked differently in Safari vs Chrome due to canvas compositing differences. I used globalCompositeOperation = 'destination-in' with a fallback for Safari.

Challenge 2: CSV Upload Memory. Initial implementation loaded the entire CSV into memory. With 10K+ row files, the server ran out of memory on Render's free tier (512MB). I switched to streaming with csv-parser library, processing 500 rows at a time using highWaterMark.

Challenge 3: Playwright Test Flakiness. Some tests failed intermittently because Render's free tier spins down after inactivity (~30s cold start). I added page.waitForLoadState('networkidle') and increased timeouts for the first request. Pass rate went from 85% to 100%.

Why This Project Impresses Interviewers

  • Shows end-to-end ownership — frontend, backend, database, deployment, testing
  • Demonstrates learning progression across 3 frameworks
  • Has production-grade features — PWA, E2E tests, CI/CD, image optimization
  • Is live and deployed — interviewer can actually use it
